Hi @felixgwu,

In a local branch I factored out the random parameter generation from the transforms, and I'm using it now in a project for semantic segmentation.

The drawback of this approach is that we need to pass an extra argument to the constructor of the dataset (let's call it `generators`). The `generators` are a list of object that when called generate the random parameters for the transforms.
 Thus, in the `__getitem__`, if the `generators` were provided in the constructor, we need to call them in order to generate the parameters for the transforms, that can be used for both the inputs and the targets.

This requires passing a new argument to the constructor, but I think it's better than having joint transforms, because we light want to apply some individual transforms before / after the joint transforms, which would not be possible in this setup.

I think the last option is the closest to what I was thinking and but perhaps with divided functionality. 

```__aexit__``` would handle abnormal termination (errors directly in the block and uncaught nursery exceptions) while assuring all tasks are canceled when leaving.

 ```supervise()``` would handle the normal nursery functionality (blocking until task completion, reaping, etc).

While I can see the need to cancel all tasks as we leave the scope in ```__aexit___```, I could foresee wanting to catch exceptions without automatically canceling in ```supervise```. In a server scenario, we would have built up a lot of state in our tasks that would be a pain to recreate (even if we can catch the cancellations and persist).  It would be slick to be able to catch an exception, deal with it and then call supervise again without having to exit the nursery and recreate all of our tasks.

I am new to this style of programming (and really enjoying it :-) but my naive understanding of "with" is that ```__aexit__``` is more for cleaning up than blocking indefinitely to do work.  It sometimes feels a bit misleading to have so much processing going on in a statement that can't actually be viewed in my code (part of why tracebacks on the seemingly unrelated last lines in the scope were confusing me early on).

Caffe implement BatchNorm by BN + Scale layer.

BN = ( X - mean ) / (sqrt(var))

in the opset version 6, the converter will generate below case

X = Conv ...              with shape_X = (N, C, H, W)
Y = BN (X, scale(1), bias(0), mean, var)
Z = Mul(Y, gamma ,axis=1, broadcast=1)
W = Add(Z, beta, axis=1, broadcast=1)

OK, so as of 81c0ed328141cedc1a796f9178a6df80d671ac76, let's go over the good and the bad.

First, the good. I can do the following things with a str object on Python 2 (or a bytes object on Python 3):

- Initialize a repository with a non-UTF-8 path.
- Create a commit with non-UTF-8 author, committer, or message.
- Create a tag with a non-UTF-8 name, tagger, or message.
- Initialize an index with a non-UTF-8 path.
- Add an entry to the index with a non-UTF-8 path.
- Test if an index contains an entry whose name isn't in UTF-8.
- Look up an index entry with a non-UTF-8 name.
- Set an index entry with a non-UTF-8 name.
- Look up a reference with a non-UTF-8 name.
- Create a reference with a non-UTF-8 name.
- Create a symbolic reference with a non-UTF-8 target.
- Rename a reference to a non-UTF-8 name.
- Change a symbolic reference's target to a non-UTF-8 name.
- Test if a tree contains an entry whose name isn't in UTF-8.
- Look up a tree entry with a non-UTF-8 name.

Great, that matches Git's behavior. However, what about inspecting existing things?

- On a commit, if `message_encoding` is wrong, I'll get a `UnicodeDecodeError` when trying to access `.committer`/`.author`/`.message`.
- If I call `Repository.listall_references()` I'll get a `UnicodeDecodeError` if one of the reference names isn't UTF-8-encoded.
- Same with `Repository.path` and `Repository.workdir`.
- Same with `TreeEntry.name`.
- Same with `Tag.name` and `Tag.message`.
- Same with `IndexEntry.path`.
- Same with `Reference.name` and `Reference.target.

This doesn't match Git's behavior. There are potentially things I can't access at all through pygit2!

Another thing to think about: What if I'm getting data from multiple sources, like both pygit2 and Git itself? How can I consistently deal with both of them? For example, say I have a Git hook that reads off old SHA/new SHA/ref name lines from `sys.stdin` and I want to keep track of how the refs Git told me about relate to all the refs in the repository. Well, if a ref isn't ASCII-encoded, I won't be able to match it up with the same ref `Repository.listall_references()` gave me. If I assume all refs are UTF-8-encoded, that would get me closer, but if one isn't, I'll get an error when I try to decode it.

I don't think pygit2 is the right place to try to force Git--an encoding-agnostic system--into a Unicode-aware system. I would expect it, as a simple binding to libgit2, to give me the same data I'd get back by using libgit2 directly or by using Git itself. If I want to force everything to be Unicode, I can do that on my own (and I probably should do that on my own, because there isn't a single, correct way to do it).

My suggestion would be to stick to `str` on Python 2 and `bytes` on Python 3. I would also suggest making `py_str_to_git_oid` support hexadecimal object IDs with `str` objects on Python 2 and `bytes` objects on Python 3. This makes it easier for me to take output from Git (or other things) and plug it directly into pygit2. (That would complicate supporting binary object ID lookups, however.)

You could also try to take Python 3's lead of using the `surrogateescape` codec everywhere and providing Unicode and non-Unicode versions of every API, but I think that will end up being a lot more complicated.
